questions.qText: What are the 4 pillars of OOP with a real example?

blocks.text: Encapsulation — hide state behind methods (private fields + validated setters). Inheritance — StripeGateway extends PaymentGateway. Polymorphism — gateway.charge() works for any implementation. Abstraction — expose an interface (what) and hide the implementation (how).

questions.qText: == vs equals(), and the Integer cache trap.

blocks.text: == compares references; equals() compares logical content when overridden. For objects/Strings use equals(). Trap: Integer a=127,b=127; a==b is true (cache −128..127) but 128==128 is false.

questions.qText: Why is String immutable?

blocks.text: Security (holds paths/credentials), thread-safety without locks, safe HashMap keys (cached hashCode), and the string pool sharing literals to save memory. Any modification creates a new object; use StringBuilder for loops.

questions.qText: How does HashMap work internally (Java 8+)?

blocks.text: Array of buckets; index = spread(hashCode) & (capacity-1). Collisions form a linked list that treeifies to a red-black tree past 8 entries. Resizes (doubles) when size > capacity × 0.75, then rehashes. Average O(1) get/put.

questions.qText: Explain Java 8 Streams: intermediate vs terminal, map vs flatMap.

blocks.text: Intermediate ops (map, filter, sorted) are lazy and build a pipeline; terminal ops (collect, reduce, count) trigger execution. map is 1→1; flatMap is 1→many and flattens nested streams.

questions.qText: Checked vs unchecked exceptions?

blocks.text: Checked (extends Exception) are compiler-enforced, for recoverable external failures. Unchecked (extends RuntimeException) are programming errors, not forced. Modern practice: favor unchecked + a global @RestControllerAdvice handler at the API boundary.

questions.qText: What are the concurrency basics you'd mention (volatile, synchronized, Atomic)?

blocks.text: synchronized gives mutual exclusion + visibility (happens-before). volatile gives visibility only (good for flags, not count++). AtomicInteger uses lock-free CAS for correct fast counters. For pooled executors prefer ExecutorService over raw threads; virtual threads (Java 21) scale blocking I/O code.

questions.qText: What are IoC and Dependency Injection, and which injection type is best?

blocks.text: IoC means the framework controls object creation/wiring; DI supplies dependencies to a class instead of it constructing them. Prefer constructor injection: immutable, required deps explicit, easy to unit-test with mocks.

questions.qText: What does @SpringBootApplication do and how does auto-configuration work?

blocks.text: It combines @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan. Auto-config applies @Conditional-guarded configs based on the classpath and your beans (@ConditionalOnClass/OnMissingBean), so your own beans win. Debug with --debug conditions report.

questions.qText: Explain how @Transactional works internally.

blocks.text: Spring wraps the bean in a proxy that begins a transaction, runs the method, and commits on success / rolls back on RuntimeException. Consequences: self-invocation bypasses the proxy (no transaction), only public methods are advised, checked exceptions don't roll back unless rollbackFor is set, and long transactions during external calls exhaust the connection pool.

questions.qText: How do you secure REST APIs with JWT in Spring Security?

blocks.text: A OncePerRequestFilter extracts the Bearer token, validates signature/expiry, builds an Authentication, and stores it in SecurityContextHolder; route rules / @PreAuthorize enforce access. Set SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS. Login issues a short access token + refresh token.

questions.qText: How do you handle exceptions globally?

blocks.text: @RestControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler methods maps exceptions to a consistent error body (code, message, traceId) and status. @Valid failures produce MethodArgumentNotValidException → 400 with field errors. Never leak stack traces.

questions.qText: How do you configure CORS for a Spring API + React frontend?

blocks.text: Define allowed origins/methods/headers globally via a CorsConfigurationSource bean (or WebMvcConfigurer). Restrict origins to your frontend domains, allow credentials only if using cookies, and remember the browser sends a preflight OPTIONS for non-simple requests.

questions.qText: useState vs useEffect — and the useEffect cleanup?

blocks.text: useState holds component state and re-renders on change. useEffect runs side effects after render, controlled by a dependency array; return a cleanup function to cancel subscriptions/timers before the next run or unmount.

questions.qText: How do you call a Spring Boot API from React and handle loading/errors?

blocks.text: Fetch in an effect (or better, use TanStack Query for caching, retries, and loading/error states). Track loading and error, send the JWT in the Authorization header, and handle 401 by redirecting to login or refreshing the token.

questions.qText: What are keys in React lists and why do they matter?

blocks.text: Keys give list items stable identity so React can match elements between renders and update efficiently. Use a stable unique id, never the array index for dynamic lists (it causes wrong state reuse and bugs on reorder/insert).

questions.qText: useMemo vs useCallback vs React.memo?

blocks.text: useMemo caches a computed value; useCallback caches a function identity; React.memo skips re-rendering a child if its props are shallow-equal. Use them to fix measured re-render problems (especially stable callbacks passed to memoized children) — don't sprinkle prematurely.

questions.qText: Controlled vs uncontrolled components?

blocks.text: Controlled: form value lives in React state (value + onChange) — single source of truth, easy validation. Uncontrolled: the DOM holds the value, read via a ref. Controlled is the default; libraries like React Hook Form reduce re-renders for big forms.

questions.qText: What is the N+1 problem in JPA and how do you fix it?

blocks.text: Fetching N entities then lazily accessing an association fires 1+N queries. Fix with JOIN FETCH (JPQL), @EntityGraph, or batch fetching; detect by logging SQL and counting queries in tests.

questions.qText: LAZY vs EAGER fetching — what's the rule?

blocks.text: LAZY loads an association only when accessed; EAGER always loads it. @ManyToOne defaults EAGER (usually change it), @OneToMany defaults LAZY. Rule: make everything LAZY and fetch exactly what a query needs with JOIN FETCH/EntityGraph to avoid over-fetching and LazyInitializationException.

questions.qText: Explain ACID and isolation levels briefly.

blocks.text: ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Isolation levels trade correctness for concurrency: READ COMMITTED (no dirty reads) → REPEATABLE READ (MySQL default, stable rows) → SERIALIZABLE (full isolation, lowest concurrency). Use versioning/locking where money is involved.

questions.qText: Write a query for the second highest salary.

blocks.text: Use a subquery or a window function (DENSE_RANK) to handle ties.

lines: SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) rnk FROM emp

questions.qText: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking?

blocks.text: Optimistic (@Version) fails the update if the row changed since read — great for low contention, retry on conflict. Pessimistic (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) blocks other writers — for hot rows like a wallet balance. Choose by contention and cost of a retry.

questions.qText: Design a URL shortener.

blocks.text: POST long→short, GET short→301 redirect. Generate keys as base62 of a unique id (or random + collision check). Read-heavy, so front the DB with a Redis cache and read replicas. Discuss QPS/storage estimates and 301 vs 302 (caching vs analytics).

questions.qText: Monolith vs microservices — the honest trade-off?

blocks.text: Microservices give independent deploy/scale and fault isolation at the cost of network complexity, distributed transactions, and observability overhead. A modular monolith is the right starting point for most teams; split out services when team size or scaling pain justifies it.

questions.qText: Where would you add caching in a full-stack app, and what would you never cache?

blocks.text: Cache read-heavy, slow-changing data: product catalog, config, sessions, computed views (short TTL). Never cache (or key carefully by user): cart, inventory at checkout, payment status, or anything personalized. Prefer delete-on-write + TTL to bound staleness.

questions.qText: Two Sum in Java.

blocks.text: HashMap of value→index, check complement in one pass. O(n) time and space.

lines: if (m.containsKey(need)) return new int[]{ m.get(need), i };

questions.qText: Reverse a linked list (iterative).

blocks.text: Walk the list re-pointing next to the previous node. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines: head.next = prev; prev = head; head = next;

questions.qText: Valid parentheses.

blocks.text: Push openers on a stack; on a closer pop and match; valid iff the stack is empty at the end. O(n).

questions.qText: Implement an LRU cache in Java.

blocks.text: Quickest: extend LinkedHashMap with access order and removeEldestEntry. In an interview, also explain the manual HashMap + doubly-linked-list design giving O(1) get/put.

lines: LRU(int cap){ super(cap, 0.75f, true); this.cap = cap; }
protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K,V> e){ return size() > cap; }

questions.qText: You get LazyInitializationException in production. What happened and how do you fix it?

blocks.text: You accessed a LAZY association after the persistence context (transaction/session) closed — e.g., in the controller or during JSON serialization. Fix by fetching what you need inside the transaction (JOIN FETCH/@EntityGraph) and returning a DTO, not the entity. Avoid Open-Session-In-View as a crutch.

questions.qText: A CORS error blocks the React app from calling the API. Diagnose it.

blocks.text: The browser blocks the response because the server didn't return the right Access-Control-Allow-Origin (and preflight) headers. Configure CORS on the backend for the frontend origin, allow the needed methods/headers, and set allow-credentials only if using cookies. It's a server-config issue, not a frontend bug.

questions.qText: An endpoint's p99 latency spiked after a deploy. Steps?

blocks.text: Roll back if impact is high, then diff the deploy. Check traces for where time goes (DB? external call? GC?). Common causes: an introduced N+1, a missing index on a new query, connection-pool shrink, or GC pauses from a heap change. Show a method, not guesses.

questions.qText: Tell me about yourself (Java full-stack).

blocks.text: Lead with years + domain, then both sides you own (Spring Boot APIs + React UI), a measurable win, and why this role. Keep it 45–60 seconds and end pointed at the job.

questions.qText: How do you keep frontend and backend in sync as a full-stack dev?

blocks.text: Share a contract: OpenAPI/Swagger for the API, generate types for the frontend, version endpoints, and keep changes backward-compatible. Talk about how you coordinate DTO changes and test both layers (MockMvc on backend, React Testing Library on frontend).

questions.qText: Describe a challenging bug you fixed end-to-end.

blocks.text: STAR: the symptom, how you traced it across the stack (browser network tab → API logs → DB query), the root cause, the fix, and the prevention you added. Full-stack roles value the ability to follow a problem across boundaries.

questions.qText: Do you have questions for us?

blocks.text: Yes: 'How is work split between frontend and backend on this team?', 'What does the first 90 days look like?', 'How do you handle incidents and on-call?' Shows ownership and curiosity.
